podcasts.name: Moody's Podcasts
podcasts.title: Effective CDD for banks
podcasts.excerpt: Customer Due Diligence (CDD) is more than a regulatory checkbox—it can be a critical defense against financial crime and a cornerstone of trust in banking. In this episode of Risk Reframed, Moody’s Jill DeWitt, and PwC’s Jim Daly join host Alex Pillow to break down what CDD really means for banks, how it works day-to-day, and why it matters. They answer key questions like: What are the main components of a strong CDD program? How can working in siloes impact the effectiveness of CDD teams? Why is it critical to train teams on how to do CDD, and why it matters? Which regulations shape how banks approach CDD, and what effects do they have on day-to-day operations? What common pitfalls should banks rethink when it comes to CDD? Additional resources: ACAMS website Global Coalition to Fight Financial Crime website Moody’s blog: Customer due diligence (CDD) in banks | Risk management and financial crime compliance To learn more about Moody’s please visit our website or get in touch — we would love to hear from you. Hosted by Simplecast, an AdsWizz company. See pcm.adswizz.com for information about our collection and use of personal data for advertising.
podcasts.transcript: By downloading or listening to this podcast, you are agreeing to Moody's legal terms and conditions found@moody's.com disclaimer including that the information provided is not investment or financial advice and that Moody's will not be liable for losses arising from your use of the information. This is Risk Reframed, the show where we bring you the leading risk experts from Moody's, our partners and the wider ecosystem to discuss the risks organizations face, how they're interconnected and ultimately what you, the risk professional, can do about them. Jim, welcome. And Jill, welcome back. How are you both doing? Well, Great to be here today. Great, thank you. Great. Jill has actually had the pleasure of once being the co host and interviewing me, but people will be glad to hear that. We're actually going to hear someone with expertise in her and yourself, Jim, today and we're going to talk about CDD specifically in banks and I think with a particular focus given your guys background on location, particularly in America where there are many, many types of banks and CDD can mean different things. I want to start our conversation just, you know, as normal, the normal way I do on these podcasts with the like, the basics, the foundations and then we'll get more into the nuance as we go. But if I was just to say what are the component parts of a solid DDD program? Is that there a way you'd break that down, Jim? Sure. You know, I think a solid or an effective DDD program or KYC program more broadly has in my mind four components and one is meeting all legal and regulatory requirements, specifically Customer Information program or cip. Two, gathering adequate information to make risk based decisions. Three, to use those risk based knowledge to conduct periodic ongoing monitoring. And four, and this isn't necessarily tied to the AML side, but to be effective I think you need to execute in a manner that is most frictionless for customers. Jill, would you, I mean would you agree with that? Would you add anything to that on top? No, I just think, I think that frictionless to customers is also really critical. There's a fine balance between gathering and monitoring the right information versus always going back to the customer and asking, you know, questions. There's a fine balance there. And so I think that's also a very critical element to it. We don't want to bog our customers down with the customer due diligence questions, but we also want to make sure we have a good handling and we have the right information. We need to effectively apply a risk based approach across all financial institutions. And I think we'll come back to that, the start of the why. And there are different whys to due diligence, I think. But I'll go to the how first. How do these programs typically operate in banks specifically? Because there are different ways you can go about achieving these different components. Right. So Jim, maybe if I come back to you initially, like you've worked both in house and then as an external sort of consultant helping banks sort of put these programs in place and remediate things when needed, how have you typically seen them done? Well, at the first level there needs to be a list of policy and list of requirements that are established generally by a second line compliance program. But more broadly it could also be defined by the institution and it's bored. So for instance, an institution may make a decision that they don't want a certain class of customer to be part of their institution. Maybe someone in a ultra high risk industry presents something as an extractive industry, cannabis related industry, adult entertainment. So those decisions are generally made at the institutional senior management board level. And then incorporating those at the second line compliance level will really develop a KYC or CDD policy that will define types of customers that are higher risk and maybe need specialized due diligence. They will also help build out a customer risk rating module. And now what this is differs greatly from institution to institution. Some may be a simple binary excel that there's a couple of variations. Others may be a complex model that needs to go through model validation on an annual basis and is multifactored and and updating in a real time basis. So those are key baseline components. Then you involve the first line or sometimes it's an operations back office who is collecting this information for the customers as they onboard. This could also be done through digital means. If that's the acquisition measure. Once that baseline information is there, there's some verification of the information that the customer has provided. The customer is officially onboarded and then they're part of your broader ecosystem. They have a risk rating and now that risk rating is subject to generally a periodic review cycle. This has changed over the past few years is how these are done. Traditionally you would see high every year, medium every two years, low every three years. That's gone away in some ways that some institutions are using an ultra high every six months. The high every year has stayed around, but the medium has gone down in some places to three years and the low in some places gone down to five years, seven years or never. It's all trigger based or periodic review based on other factors that aren't necessarily a timeline situation. So that's where the second line will necessarily come back in and be involved in some of those reviews. And you know, if you have an issue where a customer needs to be off boarded, this is where the all come together. The first line, the second line, potentially management on the ongoing maintenance of a customer if they presented a significant risk to the institution. Okay, so you kind of got the institution strategy needs to be set first of what they want to go after, what they don't. Therefore the risk appetite is going to derive from that. Then have your policy, then you'll have sort of your process of how you're going to go about acquiring customers, how you embed these controls in and then you define within your risk rating system how often you're going to periodic the review, as you said, or trigger based or perpetual if you're just going to monitor everything all the time for other reasons. Jim made a very good point. Yeah, I wanted to put this up. One of the things I think that was missed in his is he was talking about the institution at the board of levels, the first line and the second line really having great communication with each other and effectively updating policy or the model for the customer risk rating. And that's one of the things that I'm seeing is, you know, sometimes a disconnect is, you know, the business may change, have changed their strategy and they haven't really informed the second line. So good policy hasn't been implemented. And so I think the communication between all of those three groups is really what makes also a very effective CDD program. And I think that cannot and you're seeing that enforcement actions and all kinds of things. But that is a fundamental part of an effective CDD program is the communication between those three groups and on a routine, consistent basis. And to Jill's point, what I've seen in a number of recent MRAs, MRIAs is defining those as feedback loops. So having these defined steps and processes where there's communication between the different stakeholders, the last thing you want is your first line, your sales team to go after some new group, new product type, new. And your second line that creates the policy, puts in these controls, has no knowledge of it until after the fact it's too late. At that point, the second line and your policy team and the board, you all really need to be having these conversations before you actually after go after this market or go after the clients. So that's also. And I've seen that a lot more post Covid as different financial institutions have tried to find different revenue streams. So, but that is just critical, that feedback and communication between those three groups. Just to give one anecdote based on what Jill's describing, I had a client last year where they were looking to increase their risk profile and take on some potential riskier business areas, which would lead to potentially higher fees for them as well. So what they did at the second line and they brought us in to really helped define what those risk areas were. And they gave a list of six ones they want to highlight at the onset and say, okay, why are these riskier? What is the industry speak as to why this particular area would present higher risk for money laundering risk? Two, how does that risk manifest itself in the transactions that may be present? Three, how can the institution mitigate that risk? And for, you know, is this something that should be within their risk profile or is this something that would be beyond the scope? So, you know, we, we assisted and advised on those four components. I don't know exactly how they implemented everything, but hope we gave them the baseline to execute. This is audio only, so those that can't see, everyone's got their fingers crossed on camera. I was just gonna say a lot of what we're talking about here is the people, right? The communication. This approach of do you operate in a silo or do you have good communication? Does do things flow from one team to another in responsibility area, how are those structured as such? Is there a sort of one approach where it is very sort of, you're in charge of this piece and this piece only, and then you hand over versus is there a sort of a more of a holistic approach that you've seen banks take? And then also does that change depending on the tech they have involved? Because I think, Jim, you mentioned obviously if there's digital acquisition channels that is different to going into a branch. And then you also both talked about that frictionless customer experience might be one of the goals of a DDD program. I'm just running how that comes together. If the people are all lined up, what do we then do to operationalize this? And Jill, I'd love to hear your opinion on this, but sadly, I see in the largest, most complex institutions, where there is robust first line, robust middle office operations, and robust second line functions, where there is a pretty significant degree of siloing and you know, there are people are handling their individual areas, there may be some feedback if there are issues that are developed. But the real communication comes in those sense at the back end when there, when there is an exit or there's significant issue where senior management both on the first line and second line needs to get involved. But the day to day execution is generally done in a silo. As you move down in smaller institutions when there's less people involved and people wear many hats, there's greater intersection and greater interaction. You know, it's possible that your BSA officer sits upstairs from the branch where the people are onboarding the customers and executing them and they can just run up and say, hey, I have this customer. It's a, it's an interesting situation. Can we have a conversation while they're grabbing a coffee downstairs? So, you know, the most institutions that I work with tend to be on that larger side and tend to have some greater silos in place. I feel like with these silos, especially the larger institutions, there's a lot of pros and cons to it, right? So one of the pros is the expertise, right? If someone is just responsible for a portion of the customer, due diligence or you know, a little bit, you know, working outside of the first line, they really become an expert on that process. The legal right, which Jim mentioned is like one of the critical elements, right. Of an effective CDD program is making sure you understand your legal and regulatory obligations. They really understand that from top to bottom. Right. And so that is one of the pros unfortunately of the silos is it really brings in specialized expertise. But then one of the cons is that you don't see the who risk and you don't see the holistic, sometimes customer across the organization. So there's a lot of pros and cons. And I think that goes back to, you know, that communication is what is best for your institution and why and what risks are you able to deal with or do you have other controls in place to combat some of the risks of either siloing and, or not siloing from that perspective. So there's pros and cons to both approaches. I have my own personal opinion and sometimes I feel like the silos duplicate efforts which sometimes makes things a little operationally clunky. But like I said, but then you have the expertise there. So that, that's. Like I said, there's pros and cons to those silos. But you really see it at the largest global institutions. From my experience, yeah, I always, a lot of my time learning KYC was learning with fintech businesses who generally weren't going to try and silo. Right. They were going to try and have generalist teams but enabled by technology so they could Scale and compete and go after larger institutions, sort of book the business in whichever little piece of it they were looking to go get. And the interesting thing with them is that it's like it is the technology ultimately, like, if it's a rule that a person doesn't need to know is almost the attitude. Right? Like, it's good if they know. But really we only want people to make the in between decisions. Anything that can be boiled down to a rule, we can program it and therefore you don't need to look at it. And so like, I've always thought that that's interesting because you'll see that scale really quickly and people have built huge customer bases. But you'll also see obviously, like the, the fraction of a percentage point of people who are really upset when they've been caught out by the rule and sort of said, this wasn't, this wasn't fraud. This was just me trying to do a thing and my bank. And now I can't speak to anyone because naturally there aren't as many staff, so there's no phone number. You just, then you speak with the chat bot and crush your fingers again. So there's like, as you said, pros and cons, Jill. But yeah, I wonder if there is a best of all worlds that could be created. Well, and this is just my personal opinion, and this is probably, you know, I'm a former educator, you know, used to teach high school. Right. I think understanding the why you are doing something also helps. And sometimes if you don't, if it's all technology based and we don't spend the time training our first line in our second line and everyone in between the why we're doing something, it also, it dilutes the purpose of why we're collecting, why we're verifying. And so I think you can create the rules. Right. And use data and technology, but you also need to do enough training to train your staff on why we're doing it this way and why it's important. Let's talk about that. I mean, you kind of have both touched on it already in terms of what is the why behind cdd? And Jimmy mentioned, right, right from the start, like, meet your legal obligations because otherwise you might not be in business too long. But is there a way that you sort of phrase that if you were training, maybe take Jill's example, like the first line, how would you explain the why behind CDD to maybe someone who isn't, you know, a lawyer? By trading, like yourself, you know, the first thing is giving them the law that's in place of the regulation that's in place. So in the United States, the. Primarily there are five pillars of aml, potentially sixth with the effectiveness NPRM that's been out there. But the fifth pillar of the CDD rule came into effect as a final rule of May. Well, the final rule came in May of 2016, and then it went into effect of May of 2018. And that CDD rule has four components. To identify and verify the identity of customers. To identify and verify the identity the beneficial owners of companies. To understand the nature and purpose of customer relationships, the development customer risk profiles and to conduct ongoing monitoring. To identify and report suspicious transactions on a risk basis and to maintain and update the customer information. And so that's the baseline legal situation and to really get employees to understand what the impact is. There are many nefarious actors that are using the US Banking system to funnel the proceeds of drug trafficking, human trafficking, other illegal activities. And they are in essence, sometimes doing this under false pretenses by establishing shell companies at institutions. So this is real risk that goes on. I encountered an engagement a few years ago where someone had an unlawful money service businesses account and they were running the proceeds of drugs, prostitution, gambling through a faux account, through an institution. And there was ultimately a series of federal indictments and jail times for the parties behind this. But this is real crime. This isn't something that's hypothetical. It's not just some paper moving. It's not just some exercise in futility. You are protecting both the institution and frankly, the US marketplace from illegal actors taking advantage of the banking system. So to really emphasize their role in playing a key part of this and show the interplay where law enforcement comes and they pull records, they pull SAR records. They use AML information extensively in their investigations. So my goal with employees training is always to make it real for them and show how it impacts the broader world. Does that resonate? And then you. Yeah, and this is, I think a lot of people in this space, right? And a lot of us are very probably a little too idealistic, right? We would be the, you know, pageant contestants. They ask, what do you want? And we would explain world peace. Right? But it's kind of the same thing. Like, we really believe what we're doing with effective CDD programs is really protecting the US financial system as a whole. And that's also protecting the US population as a whole. Because these, you know, Jim mentioned the, the human trafficking, the drug trafficking, you know, all these frauds and scams that are affecting our Elderly. Right. That money has to flow through something and they're using the US Financial system to profit from these illegal and awful activities. And so an effective CDD program also really protects the US Financial system and also the entire US population. And so like I said, it's a little bit, you know, I want world peace. But most of us in the space really truly believe that is what we are doing with effective CDD programs. It's always good when sort of the passion when you speak about that. Jo, I can safely say that I don't think I'll ever be put into a pageant. So that might be. I don't think I will be other because I'm a little bit too outspoken. But if you ask a lot of people in this space, like we really believe that the work we're doing is protecting the US financial institution. And with an effective CDD program, an effective risk rating, we are really looking at the risks, mitigating the risks and hopefully finding those illicit bad actors along the way to help law enforcement with those indictments that Jeb talked about in some of those prosecutions. Well, I hope that's given everyone that's listening that maybe isn't directly involved in CDD. Maybe just a bit of a 101 of what goes on, what we're trying to aim at here and how it might come to be. Let's talk a little bit about some of the details and nuances. We've touched on some of this already, but the risk based approach kind of underpins almost everything. It feels like when we talk about this space. And Jim, you mentioned a few different areas there, right? Like somebody might say I don't want to work with the adult entertainment industry or I don't want to work with the, even the legal marijuana sort of industry because it's obviously there's a federal state thing there. You maybe talk through how the risk based approach shifts, you know, how CDD operates. Like what would be the differences when you are looking at a high risk versus the medium and what are the types of things that normally go in that risk module? And the risk based approach is the US system for anti money laundering, for sanctions. We do have a strict liability system, but everything in the US is risk based on the anti money laundering side, inclusive of CDD and broader KYC controls. So generally what we'll see is a different degree of information that's collected and sometimes this will impact the number of percentage of beneficial ownership that is collected for a party. So, you know, the standard for the CDD rule would be 25% or in the United States it differs in some of the European jurisdictions, sometimes we will see in a higher risk entity where they'll go down to 10%. Sometimes I've seen in a ultra high risk entity where they wanted anyone over 5% as a one off, so to speak. The other area we'll see is the enhanced documentation that's collected. You know, there's a certain baseline documentation that's collected for every customer. Some institutions are under what would be deemed simplified due diligence because they're publicly traded and all their information is readily available via K1s or other filings. For most customers you're getting some degree of information on the entity. It may be their corporation documents, articles, incorporation, where they're registered, things of that nature, what, what their business line is. For those that are deemed higher risk, you know, it may be a more specific set of documents. Sometimes it's based on high risk characteristics generally. Sometimes it's based on the type of institution that is a de facto high risk based on the industry in which they operate. And the other thing you'll see is the increased monitoring I already referenced, the periodic review cycle is going to be enhanced for higher risk customers. We often sometimes see that in transaction monitoring scenarios there's different thresholds or different rules that may be in place for high risk customers. And I know we use the word higher or high risk, but we generally see it those that are deemed high risk or getting this special treatment rather than medium versus low. Joe, anything you or from your experience working in the banking sector, when you looked at these different markets, was it as simple as just once it's in high risk, then that's one bucket and we do these things. Or would it, would it differ a bit more like hey, if this person's a crypto institution versus this person's a correspondent bank, maybe how would you then go about your next action? I think it really is industry specific, right? So for example, if I'm onboarding a high risk or you know, I have a high risk customer that's maybe in like logistics, right. I may be asking more questions about their supply chain versus a customer that's in crypto, I may be asking about their AML program, their controls. So it's a little bit of why they're high risk on what a lot of your next steps are, I would say. But that is that effective policy that goes back to the ED on what will make you comfortable banking or having a relationship with these higher risk customers. So I think understanding the why they're high risk. Let's get going back to that. Why? And then effectively making sure that either they're mitigating the risk or you're mitigating the risk is documented and documented well. In addition to some of those enhanced documentary requirements that Jim talked about earlier in beneficial ownership requirements. Jim, you obviously work with many institutions now as a consultant role. You get exposed to more different places rather than just in one. Is there things that people typically get wrong around risk based approach or is there bad practices without naming any names? That's not that this isn't the show for that. I'm just wondering if people sometimes think they've got a risk based approach when really they haven't and if there's any pitfalls that people can avoid. Definitely. So there's a few pitfalls that I've seen. One is the use of overly broad statements in policies. And I will cite a specific example here. I encountered one client that had a number of branches in states where marijuana was legal. And they had in their AML policy we do not bank any clients that derive income from marijuana related sales or the like. Now that could be true for they're not going to onboard someone like the Green Galley or someone who's selling, selling weed, selling cannabis directly to customers or someone who's a gross, a gross grow farm. However, they had a number of strip malls in their portfolio or you know, real estate companies that own strip malls. Many of these strip malls had significant number of cannabis stores in them. They had a number of hardware stores in their profile in areas where there are significant growth farms. So these would be deemed the two or tier three marijuana related businesses. So to have an overly broad statement that is in essence setting you up to fail because you're either going to offboard all your real estate holdings in the given areas where there are. I live in New York City now. Every business that goes out, it seems like a marijuana cannabis shop is coming in. So you know, this is not. And we're not fully legal here, we're partially legal. So in some of the west coast states it's even more bunker. So that's certainly one of the pitfalls. The other thing I've seen in terms of operationalizing the execution of this, you know, clients have these dynamic customer risk rating models which both may increase the customer risk rating over time or decrease the customer trading over time. And I've seen direct regulatory criticism for those where the model would move an entity from higher risk to medium risk. And there is not a subsequent review of the transactions and a full periodic review by a human being that would justify that move. So you know, they automatically, you know, it's been high risk, it's had a periodic view, the system says it's medium and then they just treat it as medium. What institutions really should do in that realm is have a human make a determination and then get it signed off by leadership that this, this entity is now a medium risk entity. Okay, so really that documentation, paper, trial of the judgment rather than purely computer says yes or no. It's sort of interesting to hear that. To go back to the regulation aspect, Jill, when you were last on we were talking about the Corporate Transparency act with our other another gym, actually not this gym, but a different gym. And I just wonder how you think that might impact CDD for bank, if at all. I mean we're several months further on that recording. Well, so right now most financial institutions are operating under that CDD rule that went into effect of May of 2018. And honestly, until they are told otherwise, that is what they are going to be doing. Yes, the Corporate Transparency act has passed, there are some lawsuits and it's been put on hold and we're also waiting on further regulatory guidance. So until then. Right. Most financial institutions are operating under their legal and regulatory requirement, which is the CDD rule of 2016, which went live in May of 2018. So though that's until otherwise told, I think that's what everyone's going to be operating under. Jim, unless you have any other thoughts there. Well, just some more context because I know not everyone on this podcast is as in depth on the Corporate Transparency act as you or I. So you know the Corporate Transparency act passed January 1, 2021 with a three year sunrise period and there were three large rulemakings out of that. So September of 22 defines who needs to report and what they need to report. December of 23, defined access. And then the third element which is still waiting for a notice of potential rulemaking as Jill described, is going to modify and amend the Customer Due Diligence Rule of May of 2018. We are still waiting for that. So you know, this has been in the Federal Register as coming out at least four times in the last two years and it has. None of those deadlines have been met. In addition to the lawsuits that have created further muddled the situation. I think realistically we're at a point now that it's two years from now at the very earliest that would be have an active final amendment to the CDD rule that would be in place and so every institution that I've encountered with, I've been at conferences where this has been a survey of those in the room. You know, my institution hosts a quarterly CDE roundtable where you have this as an ongoing topic. And no one is doing anything differently right now. They're keeping awareness of what's going on in the broader situation, in the broader legal in the courts, but they're taking no action to change their practices because they are still governed by the May of 2018 final rule, and they will be for the foreseeable future. Understood. And maybe it's do a different episode about those lawsuits and potential impacts. That would be a whole different topic. What about other regulatory changes? Is there anything that sort of happened recently or that is on the horizon that you think is going to affect CDD programs writ large? Well, the one thing that I earlier referenced In June of 2024, there was a notice of potential rulemaking. What's being deemed the effectiveness nprm. And the primary components of that would be making the risk assessment a sixth pillar and ensuring that the risk assessment drives the other five pillars. So, you know, while this may be done implicitly or tacitly, institutions would really have to take the results of the risk assessment and show how it is driving their CDD program, their internal controls, their training and the like. So, you know, that will be and you know, there's, there's a lot of talk about which elements of this may pass. I know there's been pushback by some of the major institutions, but if that comes into play, both incorporating the risk assessment as a required element and you know, 98% of institutions already have a risk assessment. They'd also have to incorporate the national priorities into that risk assessment and then use the risk assessment to drive the other aspects of the program. So they have to be a little more intentional and explicit about several elements here, operationally and potentially technologically, that might be where the change is. Even though they're doing a risk assessment, it's the actual how you're doing it that's going to matter. Maybe. Jill, are you about to jump in there? Yeah. I mean, it's really critical and I think a lot of it goes back to those national priorities, which as you may know, I'm kind of passionate about the human trafficking side of it. But it's also really evaluating do I have risks in these areas, how am I mitigating those risks? Right. And it's just putting on paper basically what most financial institutions are actually doing. That's my personal opinion. I'M sure Jim has other thoughts, but I, like I said, 98% of financial institutions already have a very robust risk assessment framework. It's just putting it, codifying it. And going back to what Jim said, an effective CDD program is your legal and regulatory requirements and just making this an actual requirement versus a nice to have, which most do have. I do want to go back to that question. You're saying, what, what do you think has changed? You know, and Jim mentioned this rule. I also want to mention, you know, we talked about the talk between the board of directors, you know, the first line and the second line. We have also seen that play a more critical part in enforcement actions lately about making sure the board of directors is fully aware of what's going on with your AML and CDD programs. So I've seen that this year as well. Yeah, so like that communications piece and the reporting and the dashboarding and, and all those beds. I was going to ask around, Jim, you mentioned it earlier around CDD has this risk based approach or AML as a whole has this risk based approach. Sanctions has more. This liability approach and sanctions is one example. But how would you describe if there's going to be some changes to CDD potentially or if there were changes for any reason, how does that have a sort of knock on effect to other areas of regulatory compliance? Is there anything you've sort of seen that might help an audience member think, hey, if something comes in or if we have to change something, what other departments that are also linked to this overall risk mitigation pieces of the banks, what would they need to think about? You referenced the sanction side. So the all institutions and even beyond financial institutions, corporate parties as well are generally doing some degree of screening their customers or their vendors or their suppliers. And the information that's screened is only as good as they collect. So what are they collecting and verifying from their customers as a result of their CDD program? What are they collecting? Are there beneficial owners? You know, the OFAC regulations have a 51% ownership threshold for, you know, SDN determinations and the like. So, you know, if institutions at the CDD level are not collecting this information properly and verifying this information properly, you're just setting yourself up to fail down the road, not feeding the sanctioned screening tools. You're potentially missing, you know, true hits from an SDN or other sanctions perspective. The other area would be anti bribery and corruption. You know, are you identifying part of, you know, as part of CDD practices? You're going to identify those with government ties or in the AML space, PEPs Politically Exposed Persons. You know, this is essential for any ABAC program that you know, where you have those relationships either on the supplier side or the customer side with those who have positions of government authority that would be subject to potential abuse. So, you know, it's all very an intertwined system and these are two of the areas that would be key outputs from the CDD information. And it's interesting going back to, as I mentioned, from my experience building this with technology firms, all of these things would be in one flow. One, all of these things being one tool. So it's interesting again when you start to think about where there's silos it can create, maybe the blindness maybe to people haven't, maybe they don't think about the knock on effects or hopefully they do, but maybe all this work happens in a different room or on a different screen somewhere. Whereas the smaller firms might be doing all of this at once, which gives them control, but maybe overworked at a certain point. You know, we just had a conversation with a client this past week where they have one of the leading sanctioned screening tools, but they don't have the module for adverse media checked. So this is something that would be part of their license, but they're just not utilizing that information. So, you know, to be aware of the capabilities of the tools you have, what, you know, what information you're getting. Are you getting pep information? Are you getting adverse media?
